profile.name: Jennifer
profile.role_in_household: Primary client; tends to lead financial discussions and keep the meeting on track
profile.financial_literacy_level: High—comfortable with account types, taxes, and trade-offs; asks pointed questions and notices inconsistencies
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Moderate in practice: willing to take market risk for retirement income but wants clear guardrails (cash buffer, downside explanations) before agreeing
profile.communication_style: Direct and structured; uses specific numbers when she has them, and when she doesn’t she’ll say things like “I’m not looking at the statement—ballpark…”; occasionally interrupts to correct details; minimal small talk
emotional_tendencies: calms down when there is a written plan
profile.decision_making_style: Analytical and checklist-driven; prefers to compare 2–3 options, then decide; wants rationale tied to goals (income/retirement) and taxes
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously trusting—open to guidance but expects competence, transparency on fees/risks, and follow-through
internal_biases: anchoring to prior market levels or prior interest rates
loss aversion (dislikes realizing losses even if logical)
hidden_concerns: worries about outliving assets and needing predictable retirement income
concerned about taxes and making an avoidable mistake
quietly concerned about managing finances smoothly as they age (simplicity and continuity)

profile.name: Joseph
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner; participates actively but often lets Jennifer drive final decisions
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate—understands the basics (income coming in, bills going out) but mixes up terms like balance vs payment or gross vs net; prefers plain-English explanations
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: More conservative emotionally: reacts to headlines and prefers “steady” outcomes; will agree to moderate risk if he hears a clear income story and downside plan
profile.communication_style: Conversational and story-based; speaks in round numbers (“about,” “roughly,” “a couple hundred”); asks clarifying questions and sometimes circles back to the same point to confirm; uses fillers like “you know” and “I think”
emotional_tendencies: anxious about debt or high interest rates
relieved by predictable monthly cash flow
profile.decision_making_style: Consensus-seeking; wants Jennifer comfortable first, then he signs on; prefers one clear recommendation rather than many choices
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Generally trusting once rapport is established; looks for patience and clarity more than technical depth
internal_biases: recency bias (recent market news feels more important than long-term averages)
mental accounting (separates ‘rental money’ from ‘investment money’ in his head)
hidden_concerns: worries about keeping up with monthly obligations without feeling constrained
concerned about the rental income being dependable without wanting to get into operational details
doesn’t want to feel like a burden if health changes
